title: Hormuz Closed Again as Iran Blinks on Talks, Israel and Hezbollah Agree Ceasefire, Ukraine Brings the War to Moscow
subhead: A day of whiplash: Iran re-closed the Strait of Hormuz, then Israel and Hezbollah agreed to a ceasefire; Ukraine struck Moscow's refinery in its largest drone attack of the war; the EU moved toward a forced diversification law on China; and South Korea's new president pressed Trump on a phased North Korea approach.

sections.heading: Iran Re-Closes Hormuz, Demands Insurance and Conditions for Transit
sections.body:
Iran's IRGC announced over maritime radio channels on Friday that the Strait of Hormuz was closed again, just one day after the US lifted its two-month naval blockade of Iranian ports under the MOU. The IRGC cited Israel's continued military operations in Lebanon and the ongoing presence of US forces in the region as justification. The statement warned all vessels to stay away from the strait and threatened to target any ship that defied the order. The move came the same day planned US-Iran nuclear talks in Switzerland were cancelled — Vice President JD Vance's trip to Bürgenstock was called off, with the White House citing logistical issues, though Iran-linked media reported Tehran was suspending its delegation over Israeli strikes in Lebanon.

Even before the formal re-closure, Iran's new Persian Gulf Strait Authority (PGSA) issued a document, seen by CBS News, requiring all vessels transiting Hormuz to hold insurance approved by Tehran and follow Iran-designated routes around Larak Island. The PGSA said it reserved the right to introduce insurance fees in the future. Oil prices had already dropped sharply on Thursday as tankers — including three Saudi-flagged vessels carrying 6 million barrels — transited the strait under the deal, pushing Brent to a 30% decline from its April peak. But by Friday, Brent stabilized around $79.78, still down roughly 8% for the week, as the re-closure and postponed talks injected fresh uncertainty. Analyst Tim Waterer of KCM Trade told Republic World that 'some skepticism' was returning about how quickly the agreement would deliver real changes on the ground.
sections.whyItMatters: Iran's re-closure of Hormuz on the same day the US lifted its blockade reveals how quickly the deal's core promise — open transit — can be reversed. The PGSA's insurance requirement is an attempt to establish permanent regulatory control over the waterway, toll-free for now but with the architecture for future fees in place. If Iran keeps transit conditional on Israeli withdrawal from Lebanon, the Hormuz clause becomes a hostage to a conflict the MOU never resolved.

sections.heading: Israel and Hezbollah Agree Ceasefire as Lebanon Toll Reaches 47
sections.body:
Israel and Hezbollah agreed to a ceasefire beginning at 4pm local time on Friday, according to senior US officials cited by CBS News and Reuters. President Trump told NBC News he personally asked Israeli officials to agree to the truce, calling it 'little icing on the cake' of the broader US-Iran deal. Israel's ambassador to the US, Yechiel Leiter, said Israel halted all offensive operations at 11:30am local time and remained 'firmly committed to an immediate ceasefire,' while Hezbollah told Reuters it would observe the truce if Israel withdrew from Lebanese territory entirely.

But the ceasefire came after a bloody day: at least 47 people were killed and 97 wounded by Israeli airstrikes and bombardments in Lebanon on Friday, according to the Lebanese health ministry. The dead included at least seven women and two children. Israel has no plans to leave the security zone it established in southern Lebanon, Leiter said — a position Hezbollah calls a violation of the MOU. Secretary of State Marco Rubio told Lebanon's President Joseph Aoun that bilateral negotiations between Israel and Lebanon are scheduled for June 23-25 in Washington. Mediators from Pakistan, Saudi Arabia, Turkey, and Egypt are due to meet in Cairo on Sunday to discuss regional developments.
sections.whyItMatters: The ceasefire is real but thin. Israel is not withdrawing from its security zone in southern Lebanon, and Hezbollah considers any continued Israeli presence a violation. The gap between the diplomatic text (an 'immediate and permanent' end to hostilities) and the reality on the ground (Israeli forces still occupying Lebanese territory) is where this deal is most likely to fracture.

sections.heading: Ukraine's Moscow Refinery Strike Exposes Russian Air Defense Gaps
sections.body:
Ukraine struck the Moscow Oil Refinery for the second time in a week on Thursday, in what AP described as one of the largest drone attacks on the Russian capital since the full-scale invasion began. The refinery — the largest fuel supplier to the Moscow region, processing roughly 12 million tons per year — sustained significant damage, with an oil tank lid launched hundreds of feet into the air by the blast. Flights at four Moscow airports were disrupted, with over 500 delayed or canceled, according to Kommersant. Moscow Mayor Sergei Sobyanin said the fire was 'largely contained' hours afterward.

Forbes analysis identified at least six drone types in the attack — AN-196 Lyutyi, Fire Point FP-1, Morak, Bars jet-powered drones, Bobr, and possibly the new Sichen — alongside decoys. Russian air defense, including Pantsir systems mounted on new towers around the capital, proved unable to stop the assault. Russia's Defense Ministry claimed it shot down 555 drones overnight, roughly double the number Ukraine's air force says Russia launched at Ukraine the same night. State Duma speaker Vyacheslav Volodin warned that Russia would respond with 'more powerful weapons,' while some Russian nationalists, including Konstantin Malofeev, publicly called for the use of nuclear weapons. Zelenskyy framed the attack as bringing the war home: 'If Ukraine is going to burn, your Moscow will burn too.'
sections.whyItMatters: This is not a symbolic strike. Hitting the Moscow refinery twice in a week and disabling a key distillation unit degrades Russia's domestic fuel supply at scale — one in four Russian gas stations has introduced purchase restrictions, according to independent outlet Agentstvo. The attack also demonstrated that Moscow's layered air defenses cannot reliably intercept mass drone raids, a finding with implications far beyond this war.

sections.heading: EU Summit: Sanctions Extended, Diversification Law Proposed, Fast-Track for Ukraine Blocked
sections.body:
At the June 18-19 European Council summit in Brussels, EU leaders agreed to extend sanctions on Russia for a full 12 months — the first time the bloc has renewed its package for a year rather than the usual six months. Commission President von der Leyen called it a strong signal and said work on the 21st sanctions package would continue. European Council President Antonio Costa said all 27 leaders endorsed joint conclusions on Ukraine for the first time since December 2024, and announced a diplomatic channel through his office to facilitate direct EU-Russia communication — though German Chancellor Friedrich Merz cautioned that there was 'no need for further decisions right now' on Costa's role in peace talks.

On the economic front, Trade Commissioner Maroš Šefčovič announced that the European Commission will propose a 'diversification instrument' — a new law requiring companies in sensitive sectors to source from at least three different suppliers, modeled on the Energy Union. The EU relies on China for over 90% of its rare earth supply and ran a €359 billion goods trade deficit with Beijing in 2025. The summit conclusions also discussed de-risking from China and targeting Chinese chemicals, metals, and clean-energy technology with potential tariffs. However, Hungary's Viktor Orbán blocked the acceleration clause for Ukraine's EU membership bid, according to Kyiv Post, and Russia said it was 'open to dialogue' with Europe but would not accept 'ultimatums.'
sections.whyItMatters: The EU is making tangible moves toward supply chain independence from China, but the €360 billion trade deficit and Germany's reluctance to confront Beijing mean the diversification law's teeth are still being designed. On Ukraine, the year-long sanctions renewal is significant, but Orbán's veto on fast-track membership shows that unanimity remains the EU's weakest structural constraint.

sections.heading: South Korea's Lee Pushes Phased North Korea Approach; China Rebukes US Nuclear Talks
sections.body:
South Korean President Lee Jae Myung, speaking after his first meeting with Trump at the G7 summit in Évian, said Trump signaled a 'renewed focus' on North Korea and was open to considering a phased approach to Pyongyang's nuclear issue. Lee told Trump that sanctions on North Korea have become 'ineffective' due to Russia's military cooperation with the regime, according to Reuters and MSN. Lee's office said the two leaders had a 'lengthy conversation' at a G7 dinner, and Lee urged Trump not to treat the North Korean nuclear problem the same way as other proliferation issues.

Separately, China's Foreign Ministry spokesman Lin Jian condemned the recent US-Japan Extended Deterrence Dialogue and the sixth US-South Korea Nuclear Consultative Group meeting, calling expanded nuclear deterrence cooperation a 'product of the Cold War' that 'increases the risks of nuclear proliferation and nuclear conflict.' Lin reserved his sharpest criticism for Japan, accusing Tokyo of making 'dangerous remarks' about potentially acquiring nuclear weapons, and urged Washington to 'abandon its Cold War mentality.' The Pentagon also quietly renamed USINDOPACOM back to USPACOM — reverting the 2018 rebranding that added 'Indo' to signal expanded cooperation with India — a move the Honolulu Star-Advertiser reported reflects fading US-India strategic trust.
sections.whyItMatters: Lee's push for a phased approach signals that Seoul recognizes the current sanctions regime has been hollowed out by Russia-North Korea cooperation. Meanwhile, the USPACOM renaming is a quiet but real signal: the Trump administration is pulling back from the Indo-Pacific framework that was designed to bind India into a containment architecture against China. China's rebuke of the deterrence talks is expected, but the simultaneous downgrading of the India dimension of US strategy makes Beijing's objection more pointed.

bottomLine: Friday was a day of contradictions that the deal's architects have not resolved. Iran re-closed Hormuz and set up a transit-permission regime that gives Tehran lasting leverage over the waterway. Israel and Hezbollah agreed to stop shooting, but Israel is not leaving southern Lebanon — a gap that could collapse the ceasefire within days. Ukraine demonstrated that mass drone raids can penetrate Moscow's air defenses, and Russia's response has been threats of escalation, not negotiation. The EU took real steps on Russia sanctions and China diversification, but its unity on Ukraine membership remains blocked by Hungary. And in Asia, the strategic geometry is shifting: Seoul wants a new North Korea approach while Washington quietly reverts its Pacific command name, signaling a less ambitious posture toward India. The MOU is not dead, but it is being tested faster than its drafters anticipated.
